content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
October 19,
1994
BY Facsimile and Mail
Kenneth A. Wilson,
Esg.
Meyers,
Nave, Riback, Silver
& Wilson
555 Fifth Street, Suite 230
Santa Rosa,
CA 95401
Re:
Your Request For Advice
Our File No.
1—94-307
Dear Mr. Wilson:
We write in response to your September 20,
1994 request for
advice regarding the responsibilities of Economic Development
Officer John Holt under the Political Reform Act (the “Act”) •1
QUESTIONS
Are members of the City of Healdsburg’s Economic Development
Commission public officials under the Act?
CONCLUSIONS
No.
The Economic Development Comluission is not a decision—
making body at this time.
If the Economic Development Commission
makes substantive recommendations which over an extended period of
time are regularly approved without significant modification, then
it will be considered a decisionmaking body and its members will
be subject to the conflict of interest provisions of the Act.
FACTS
In June of 1994 the City of Healdsburg created. an Economic
Development Commission
(“EDC”)
composed of two Economic
Development Officers.
The EDC mission is to ensure the retention
and development of business in the city.
The EDC is to accomplish
this mission through the following actions:
(1)
informing
potential and existing business enterprises in the city of the
city’s requirements and procedures;
(2)
making recommendations to
1
Government Code Sections 81000-9 1015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800
•
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


File No.
1—94—307
Page 2
the city council regarding changes in operating procedures and
policies;
(3) assisting existing and new businesses in the
approval process;
(4) developing an inventory of services
available from local businesses and facilitating the
identification of specific services typically needed by all
businesses;
(5)
educating the community on the impact of
businesses in the community;
(6)
developing specific products to
be made available to businesses to accomplish the mission of the
EDC;
(7)
developing a marketing and strategy program for the city;
(8) establishing press relations;
(9) attracting non-local
businesses to the city;
(10) responding to local businesses as
needed;
(11)
communicate findings to city council and chamber of
commerce by regularly attending meetings; and
(12) making written
monthly reports to the city council.
Mr. John Holt has been hired as an Economic Development
Officer
(“EDO”).
The city considers Mr. Holt to be a consultant.
Mr. Holt owns a personal residence; a 39.9 percent interest in
Plaza Square Associates,
a general partnership which owns and
manages real property;
a 35 percent interest in Straylight, Inc.,
a corporation which acts as general partner in a real estate
limited partnership;
a 25 percent interest in an unnamed joint
venture to develop a new hotel and retail complex; and a less than
5 percent interest in Pebbles
& Bam Bam,
a real estate
partnership.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from
making, participating in making,
or otherwise attempting to
influence a governmental decision in which that official knows or
has reason to know that he has a financial interest.
The Act’s
provisions are only applicable to public officials who make
governmental decisions.
Accordingly,
we begin our analysis at the
definitional stage.
Public Official:
The term “public official” is broadly defined under the Act
to include every member, officer,
employee and consultant of state
or local government.
(Section 82048.)
Regulation 18700(a)
further defines the term “public official” to include salaried and
unsalaried members of boards or commissions that possess decision—
making authority.
Therefore, whether or not the Economic
Development Officer of Healdsburg would be a public official
subject to the provisions of the Act would depend on whether or
not the EDC has decisionmaking authority.
Governmental Decision:
Under Commission Regulation 18700(a) (1),
a member of a board
or commission possesses decision—making authority whenever the
board or commission makes final governmental decisions,
compels or
prevents governmental decisions by its exclusive power to initiate


File No.
1—94—307
Page
3
a decision or by a veto power which may not be overridden, or
makes substantive recommendations which are,
and over an extended
period of time have been, regularly approved without significant
amendment or modification.
It appears that the activities of the
EDC, listed above,
do not grant that commission power to make
final governmental decisions.
Moreover, the EDC lacks exclusive
power to initiate a governmental decision and does not have any
veto power which may not be overridden.
The sole function of the
EDC which may make it a decisionmaking authority for purposes of
the Act is its power to make substantive recommendations to the
city council.
2
Where a board or commission charged with making substantive
recommendations has been newly formed and has no history of its
recommendations being regularly approved over an extended period
of time, this agency has advised that such a board or commission
is not yet a decisionmaking entity under the Act. ( Michelon
Advice Letter, 1-94-224.)
Although you stated that the EDC’s
recommendations are expected to be adopted regularly over time, it
would be inconsistent for this agency to advise that the mere
expectation would create the history which Regulation 18700(a) (1)
requires.
It will be necessary for the EDC to review its status in the
coming months to determine whether or not the factual situation
has changed such that its members would be considered public
officials who make governmental decisions under the Act.
3
As the EDC is not a decision-making entity,
Mr. Holt is not a
public official making governmental decisions at this time.
If
Mr. Halt is considered a public official making governmental
decisions at some later date, your letter accurately summarizes
2
We read the list of functions of the EDC in their ordinary
and common meaning.
Accordingly,
we do not perceive that the EDC
has discretionary power other than through the recommendations it
may make.
As you are more readily familiar with the proposed
functioning of that agency, please note that if the EDC’s
operations are in fact different from the listed functions then
this analysis would change accordingly.
In your request you note that even if the EDC does not yet
have a history of having its recommendations regularly approved,
the
EDO may be a public official by virtue of his consultant
status.
This result is not dictated by the Act.
The term
“consultant” is a term of art under the Act.
The definition is
contained in Regulation 18700(a)(2).
Nothing in the duties of the
EDO would indicate that he is a consultant for purposes of the
Act.


File No.
1—94—307
Page 4
the law regarding financial interest, foreseeability and
materiality.
Should you or Mr. Holt have additional questions
this office remains available to answer your requests.
Sincerely
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
Daniel
Counsel, Legal Division

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Are members of the City of Healdsburg’s Economic Development
Commission public officials under the Act?

CONCLUSION: No.
The Economic Development Comluission is not a decision—
making body at this time.
If the Economic Development Commission
makes substantive recommendations which over an extended period of
time are regularly approved without significant modification, then
it will be considered a decisionmaking body and its members will
be subject to the conflict of interest provisions of the Act.

CONCLUSIONS No. The Economic Development Comluission is not a decision— making body at this time. If the Economic Development Commission makes substantive recommendations which over an extended period of time are regularly approved without significant modification, then it will be considered a decisionmaking body and its members will be subject to the conflict of interest provisions of the Act. FACTS In June of 1994 the City of Healdsburg created. an Economic Development Commission (“EDC”) composed of two Economic Development Officers. The EDC mission is to ensure the retention and development of business in the city. The EDC is to accomplish this mission through the following actions: (1) informing potential and existing business enterprises in the city of the city’s requirements and procedures; (2) making recommendations to 1 Government Code Sections 81000-9 1015.
